mnemonics.title: C.P.R. for Relationship Constraints
mnemonics.content: C.P.R. = Cardinality + Participation + Referential Integrity — Remember: Constraints PROtect Relationships Properly

mnemonics.title: The 1-M-M Story
mnemonics.content: 1 Teacher Can Teach M Many Classes; M Many Students Can Enroll in 1 One Course — Think: 'One Many Many' order matters in arrows!

mnemonics.title: Total vs Partial - The Finger Test
mnemonics.content: Total Participation = All fingers down (mandatory, can't exist without the relationship); Partial Participation = Some fingers up (optional, entity can exist independently)

mnemonics.title: Parent-Child Rule
mnemonics.content: Parent Entity (referencing) must exist BEFORE Child Entity (referenced) — Remember: Parents come first, like genealogy!

mnemonics.title: ER Diagram Symbols
mnemonics.content: Double Rectangle = Weak Entity; Double Diamond = Identifying Relationship; Underline PK = Primary Key — Think: 'Weak needs Double protection'

acronyms.usage: When one entity in set A relates to exactly one entity in set B, and vice versa. Example: One Student has One Library Card
When one entity in set A relates to multiple entities in set B, but each B relates to only one A. Example: One Department has Many Employees
When multiple entities in set A can relate to multiple entities in set B. Requires a bridging table. Example: Many Students enroll in Many Courses
Attribute in a relation that references Primary Key of another relation. Enforces referential integrity between tables.
Unique identifier for each entity. Cannot be NULL. Underlined in ER diagrams.
Rule that foreign key value must match a primary key value in the referenced table or be NULL.

visualTips.visualization: Think of crow's foot (|||) as 'many' branching out like bird's feet, and single line (|) as 'one' - clean and single. The crow's foot always points to the 'many' side!
Total participation = Two solid circles connected (must exist together); Partial participation = One solid, one hollow circle (can exist independently). The hollow circle is like a 'optional' door!
Visualize a child holding hands with parent - the child (weak entity) cannot exist without the parent. Double lines = strong bond, cannot survive alone!
Think of database tables connected like bicycle chain - each link (FK) must have a matching link (PK) in the chain, or the chain breaks (referential integrity violation)

Entity sets are islands, relationship sets are bridges connecting them. Cardinality tells you bridge capacity: 1:1 = single lane each way, 1:N = highway to small road, M:N = roundabout needing extra table!

analogies.analogy: Like a Mother and her Children — One mother can have multiple children, but each child has only one biological mother.
Like Students and Courses — Many students can take many courses, just like many people can subscribe to many streaming services. Need a mediator (enrollment table)!
Like Oxygen and Life — You cannot have life without oxygen. Total participation entities are dependent like fish and water.
Like Employee and Parking Space — Having a parking space is optional for an employee; employee can exist without it.
Like a House Address — Your address (FK) must point to a real house (PK) that exists; you can't have an address for a non-existent house!

Like a Child without Parents — A weak entity (like 'Dependent' in employee database) cannot be uniquely identified without the owner entity (Employee). Just as an orphan needs identification from family.
